speaker: interviewer
text: OK we're on tape four with Mr. Ervin Chaim Greenfeld. November 15, 1995. You were telling me about the death march.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's the name I would call it. It was a most horrible march. We were driven by our own guards, but we couldn't go because the local people of this town, Loretto, they were just making blockades for us. And probably about 700 to 800 people were killed in this town. By the time I got up after I was beaten up very badly, I didn't know which way to go, but I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: One German came over to me and says, [GERMAN]? I said, [GERMAN]. I said it in German, please don't touch me. I'm a young boy and I was beaten up already. So he patted my back like a nice big brother and says, go ahead, go ahead, go ahead. The first time I saw something which was those days if somebody didn't kick you, didn't beat you up, he was a gentleman. He was one of the guys.

speaker: interviewee
text: Surely enough in the morning hours, this must have been about three, four, five o'clock in the morning, we tried to get together near the railroad in Wiener Neustadt-- this is-- before Vienna there is a town, it's called Wiener Neustadt, New Vienna. And there is a railroad station and people are coming in one by one, beaten up, bleeding, bumps, torn.

speaker: interviewer
text: There I had a friend of mine, his name was Krakowich. They were two brothers. And these brothers were so close to each other. And he says he's not going until I find my brother. I must pick up my brother. What am I going to tell my mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a horrible, horrible morning. I said to myself, thank god I don't have here no father, no mother, no brother, nothing, because those people who had their homes there many times it happens that because one of them was incapable to continue, so the other one stayed with them, they were both killed because one didn't want to leave the other one. I remember a case where a family, one of them was asking, pick me up. They made [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] a stretcher and put him in. How long it was stretching, I don't know. I remember a case. I remember a friend of mine. His name was Dini Lax, L-A-X. He was begging us, please pick me up because I was in Malthausen for many, many weeks and I don't have the strength to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the leader of our group who was not the leader, but he was the biggest. He says, Dini, we are all going to go the same way. Today, you, the next one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There's no way of escaping this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure enough, a few minutes later-- because that was the march between Malthausen and Gunskirchen, which is the last few days, three days. [INAUDIBLE]. But this happened then. And shooting of this Krakowich brother. He was completely, completely became insane. Screaming, screaming, I cannot leave before Jeno is coming. Jeno, Jeno, Jeno, Jeno, Jeno. It's Eugene. In Hungarian, they call it Jeno.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it happened that Jeno had the little bread, he had margarine. They sold a beautiful suit and a diamond ring for a piece of bread. And one had the bread. Jeno had the bread [INAUDIBLE] and he was killed. Whether he died or he was just playing half dead because of the beatings. And Bela had a big box of margarine. He didn't, but he was hysteric. He was hysteric. For weeks the man was-- and he was a genius, beautiful scholar. He was the president's son. Both of these boys were the sons of the president of the Jewish congregation in my town, Krakowiec. He was totally devastated, completely, completely. Because what is he going to say to his mother about Jeno?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, the mother was already dead in Auschwitz, months ago. But we didn't know this. We didn't know this.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to get back to you. And you said from this train station you were taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were put in to a train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: This train was supposed to take us to Malthausen. But the trip normally, probably today, takes about 35 minutes, 45 minutes. We were three days in the train. No food, nothing. We were put on a-- what do you call it? A death station. We were starting out. We were starting out. And I'm smiling because something good to me what happened over there. We were starting out and went probably a few miles, and all of the sudden, stopped because probably all the rails were not available or they were bombed or people were marching, all over marching because everybody was running away like from afar because the Russians, they didn't make any stops.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were coming full power, full steam. So we were staying in this place, call it like a dead end station. They opened up the doors and what do you think happens? Some people were dead already in the train. They took them out, buried them.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the other side of the train there was a highway and people of were marching. Hungarians, Austrians-- they were all marching away from the Hungarian border, more into the mainland of Austria. Away, toward the west. Those idiots, they thought that because the Americans were coming, pushing Germany from the west, the Russians from the east, and this space got narrower and narrower.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure enough, they used to call me a handy kid. I went under the train. I went under the train. I see the marchers. I said to myself, why don't I join them? But the way I looked, I was afraid. But I put out my hand and I begged. Maybe you have some food for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: One lady gave me, I remember-- somebody gave me a piece of-- some kind of a apple pie type of thing. And somebody gave me a box of pepper, red pepper. I don't know, red pepper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything was good, because if you would know what happened later, we did not have-- we were eating grass. And if somebody got a snail-- snail. You know, the little snail? That was the most finest food we can think of. Plain, we were not given food for days. So anybody who survived without eating three days, especially water.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to tell you something about water, but I don't know whether it's something's to--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK--

speaker: interviewee
text: Put it on the film.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could say it. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was on the trip between-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was on a trip between [INAUDIBLE] and Balf, living in a train for three days, nights. And what do you do? I mean, I know that we had some food, maybe, but there was no place. You are squeezed together like this. You're squeezed together and sitting in a place. It wasn't a train where you sit in chairs. You standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what happens, people have to-- they need their human-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] need they need. Urinating. Something like this? What do you do? First of all, there was nothing, so everybody was using whatever they had. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll never forgot that we used the same pot for your human needs. And then I'm ready to go over to the opening to spill it out somehow. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know whether this belongs on a tape. Sure enough while I was going-- I was going. I was just-- somebody gave me a kick here, push here, somebody gave me a big smack because I spilled a little bit. Sure enough, I spilled it out already on the wall. Fine. And I stayed there. It was cold. It had dried. And in the morning we were fighting to take the ice from the frozen urine, to lick it because we were so dried up.

speaker: interviewer
text: If it wouldn't be honest, I say this in front of my library, this is what happened. And I said it to my children, would you believe it if you tell this story to somebody? You are happy to find a piece of ice of the frozen-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let me now continue. This is what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was on the road before we arrived to Balf. But now we are already in Vienna under the train. And I'm going up under the train and I was begging.

speaker: interviewer
text: And coming back to my place and finding, looking for my rucksack, they call it, bag, it's not there. It's not there. Says, oh look at that, he's back. Everybody said that they took you out already, that you are gone, you know? So they took out of my things. There was nothing good there. There was no food. I had the two pairs of filling because I took it away from somebody. You know, the filling. My own father's and somebody else's filling.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was crying. I was crying, because in the Psalms, King David has a passage in the Psalms and they raffle out my clothing. He was in such terrible condition that they gave up on him and they were raffling, selling out his clothing. This is what happened to me because in Hebrew it says, [HEBREW]. They were raffling out my clothing and they were dividing my-- let's call it [YIDDISH] because I was full of lice and there's nothing there valuable, but people were just plain subhuman. It was-- the thinking was not normal. So if somebody was taken away for no reason. Later on, I've seen worse things, because in Gunskirchen you could see human flesh being sold. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sold. It was just given away for something. I've seen those cutoff bodies. You could see that people were not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, we arrived to-- and it was a horrible feeling to try to get better my junks. I got back my things and we are back in the train. And a day later then we started to go to Malthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Malthausen was a-- what shall I tell you about Malthausen? Malthausen was one of the most famous horrible places. We found over there a huge mountain like. But it was no trees, just plain people and a tent, because all the regular barracks was already full. Full.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already a get together from those places, from the smaller camps which couldn't be continued because they were all gathering together. This is like a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means--

speaker: interviewer
text: Gathering point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gathering point. From all the smaller camps in the around. There was Ebensee was also a big one. I was there was also a few days there too, Ebensee. But Malthausen was a famous place and I've seen over there one thing which I'll never forget for the rest of my life. It was the most brutal thing I've seen. There were Polish Jews we saw there. And these Polish Jews-- four years or five years they were already going through this, what we've been through in the last few, 10, 12 months. And they were just plain-- they became like animals. People are tying their shoes to their neck because somebody might take it away. A piece of bread if they see, they would ready to kill you on the spot, without questions. This is Malthausen. They had showers, but the newcomers had no room anymore in the barracks, so we were all out under the sky. And we had those watchtowers. From there, from there. Every few you could see these watchtowers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two things I have seen in Malthausen which will remain with me for this of my life. Once I have seen that they brought the big-- how do you call it? The big can of soup. That soup was probably a little water, and a little carrots. Not the regular carrots that we see, but the same carrots that they give for the cows.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a little Polish boy. He just couldn't wait to stay in line, and he went with his dish-- everybody had this dish, little dish. And he went in like this. And there was a big SS guy and he took this kid and he put his neck at the edge of that [GERMAN]. They call it [GERMAN], the big pot. And cut it off, into the soup. I've seen that. I would-- And there was another thing which I've seen in Malthausen. While we were sitting, all of the sudden, boom! One of those guys in the tower wanted to try out how he can shoot. And he shot-- and he just-- into the crowd. And so it happened that the men who got a bullet into his brain was about two yards away from me. So when the brain splashed out, I was full. And he had a brother there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that brother looked at his-- this man was shaking for the rest of the day because he was dead right away, brain dead, but the whole body was shaking like this. And the pieces of the brain, I'm telling you, was splashing all over. I remember his brother, he tried to empty out his pocket. He knew that they're going to take him away soon. Every few minutes-- few hours, you have seen those dead carriages with carrying half dead or totally dead people, they were just like pieces of-- you probably have seen this in the magazines and English films, how those camps looked with those skinny, no flesh but bones. Anyway, this man was taking out, was crying. We were also crying for him. Because I've seen the decent, the human part of it that he takes out from his brother's pocket, the pictures. And whatever he had in his pocket, he took it out, emptied it out. And a few minutes later-- But it was a whole day the man was shaking. And because just one of those animals over there wanted to try out his pistol or his gun. This is what I remember from Malthausen. We didn't work. Malthausen was already those days-- there was no work there was nothing to do. This was just a death camp waiting for the-- they were waiting that everybody should close his eyes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were there for about three weeks. And then we were given an order to go further, which was already the-- towards Gunskirchen. We didn't know what will wait for us. But it was a horrible three days of marching from Malthausen to Gunskirchen. Gunskirchen is in a forest. But the horrible thing about it was that we did not have the strength to go. No food, no water. One night, I think they were saying that the Germans were planning to bomb us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They didn't know what to do with us. So there was question that they should take German planes and bomb us. We were in a mine field. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this march was three days without food. And those guards of ours, they were just looking for the weak people. Anybody who couldn't walk was taken aside and killed. This is when my friend, Lax Dini, was begging us, please pick me up. Please pick me up because I don't have the strength to go. And I've seen these two brothers and a child-- a father and a son, and the father's brother, an uncle. Also the same thing. They picked up these boys on the stretcher and they were walking with him. I don't know how long did they go. I had another friend of mine who did not continue to go because his father couldn't go. Deutsch Tapsi was his name. Deutsch [INAUDIBLE], Uncle Edush, was the father. And his son just did not want to leave the father. He saw the father cannot continue so he said, I'm staying with my father and they'll probably kill both of us. We could hear only the shots. And this was a march. We were going and no food. This is already springtime in Austria, and you see on the fields yellow-- some kind of a grass-- they gave in Europe-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Hungarian it's called [HUNGARIAN]. It is some kind of a flowery-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's a grass, but it has beautiful, yellowish flower and it tastes good. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we saw those fields and small as I was, I was bending down and when it comes to it I running and try to fill up my little bag.

speaker: interviewer
text: Incidentally, we didn't carry anymore packages. Why? Because we didn't have the strength. However, just before sundown we picked up because the roads were full of packages-- [NON-ENGLISH], dirty shirts and pants and blankets. So I said to myself-- and it wasn't mine-- everybody made this system for themselves. Just before sundown, we picked up a blanket.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because marching in the daytime, we did not have but we only carried that filling which was the closest, which is still the closest to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were walking and just before sundown we picked up a blanket there to sleep. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And once it happened, I remember, we made a stop. And whenever there's a stop, I always ran ahead to the beginning of the group just in case I'm slowing down, I should still be within the group. Because those, they were watching for those who are slow. Those who were slow, they were just plain-- they were sentenced to death. We were all sentenced to death. But they were killing them and we could even hear that the shots. And when I was running for these flowers, for these [HUNGARIAN], and they were shooting at us, even for picking up a little grass, which is usually given to horses. They didn't want this [NON-ENGLISH]. Germans didn't want it. I will never forget another thing. Once I was standing and there was a German SS standing like this, with his open legs and his boots. And beneath, under his legs, I've seen over there a piece of rotten tomato peel. And I put my little finger there and tried to grab it out. And he sees it and just boom. With his big boots, he just stepped on it. So it wasn't the question that they didn't have what to give us. They had so much food, after the liberation less than a half a mile from the camp in Gunskirchen, we found over there warehouses where the macaronis and cans of meat. They were so deep. Tens of thousands of cans of meat. All right, they were horse meat, whatever it was. Sugar. People were dying because they did not watch themselves and they were overeating themselves. This is a well-known fact, but that is already after the whole circus was over. But we were talking about our march from Malthausen to Gunskirchen. This was three days. And I would say one third of those people who were marching through those roads-- probably, maybe not one third, but hundreds of boys were killed on the road. And we did find snails and we put a little can and somebody put a little fire and somehow tried to cook it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who has the patience to wait for it cooks? We were eating it together with the mud. It was a beautiful meal if you could find. They're just digging and fighting for it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And finally we got to Gunskirchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gunskirchen was in a forest. It was a new camp. There's no water. Were waiting for the rain to rain. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Gunskirchen was lasting not more than three weeks. If it had lasted another week, it would probably be no survivors. After the war the commanders of these camps claimed that they had a written order from Himmler to put dynamite. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That there shouldn't be-- besides being a big animal, he was also stupid because he thought that with that he would wipe, that the Americans shouldn't find any trace what happened here. Nothing happened. There's no trace. There is nothing left over. Sure enough, they claimed that-- the army commander came with this order to the American liberation army, to the commander of the American forces and showed his order. Whether he was really freed with this, I don't know, but they were talking about it that he had a paper which was in his favor because he just let us starve but he didn't explode the whole thing. In Gunskirchen was a horrible, horrible place. Every second day they brought us a little soup. And people didn't have a place to sleep. I remember people were just hanging up their blankets to the ceilings when they came. And they climbed up and they went to sleep because on the floor there was no more room. So they were hanging themselves up on the side and they never came down. They just fall asleep there. There was a Mr. Landau who had the beautiful ice cream parlor and he made beautiful cakes in my town. He passed away a few hours after the Germans, after the war was over. Also fall asleep. So did my mother in Bergen-Belsen with my sisters. And Gunskirchen, every second day they would give us soup. And the rest of the time I got over there a soup, it was-- whoever gave it out, he spilled it on my hand and it was a little hot and it dropped. I said, give me another one. No, sir. So I didn't have that soup either. This was two days before the war was over. And they were talking that the Red Cross is coming in. Once I remember that American parachuters were flying, coming down. And the Germans were shooting them in the mid air. And we were not supposed to be outside, you're supposed to be inside in the barracks. And it was a horrible place also because there was no bathrooms and people were standing in line. People were standing in line. And most of the people already had diarrhea or they were just totally-- after all, we were eating nothing. The thing that we were eating was like horse food. And somebody had the diarrhea and the other ones, some of them were just the other way around.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to pause here.
